ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'tomato soup' class (FOODON_03311737). Tamatar Dhaniya Shorba is a spiced North Indian tomato-coriander broth; mapped as closest_parent match. No exact FoodOn class exists for this dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers soups and broths as a category. No dish-specific FoodEx2 code exists for Tamatar Dhaniya Shorba; category-level mapping used as closest_parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular Indian soup entries.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Tamatar Dhaniya Shorba is approximately 250 g (one bowl), consistent with typical North Indian restaurant soup portions served in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Tomato soup, homemade / restaurant-style — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition), composite tomato-based soup entry; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy item 'Soup, tomato, home-prepared' (FDC ID 172952)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Tamatar Dhaniya Shorba exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA databases. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN tomato-based soup data and USDA SR Legacy 'Soup, tomato, home-prepared' (FDC 172952), adjusted for the lighter, less cream-based North Indian shorba style with fresh coriander. The dish is a thin, spiced broth with low fat and moderate carbohydrate from tomatoes. Nutrient values represent a best-estimate composite for a restaurant-prepared version.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of tomato-based soups and vegetable broths — analogous estimate based on 'Tomato soup' GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tamatar Dhaniya Shorba. GI of 35 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for tomato soup (GI ~35, low category) and the 2008 International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care). The dish is a thin tomato-coriander broth with low carbohydrate density (3.8 g/100 g), consistent with a low GI. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 3.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 3.3 g, confirming very low GL.

hypertension.guidance: Tamatar Dhaniya Shorba contains approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g (525 mg per 250 g serving), which represents a meaningful sodium load for hypertensive individuals; patients on sodium-restricted diets (<1500–2000 mg/day) should limit portion size or request reduced-salt preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 35 and only 3.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, Tamatar Dhaniya Shorba produces a very low glycemic load (~3.3 per serving) and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a starter or light meal component, provided sodium intake is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and lycopene-rich tomato base
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tamatar Dhaniya Shorba is low in saturated fat (0.3 g/100 g) and provides lycopene from ripe tomatoes, which is associated with cardiovascular protective effects; the moderate sodium content warrants attention in patients with heart failure or hypertensive heart disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tamatar Dhaniya Shorba (also spelled Tamater Dhania Shorba) is a classic North Indian spiced tomato and coriander broth served as a starter or light soup course in Indian restaurants across India and Singapore. The dish is prepared by simmering ripe tomatoes with aromatics including onion, garlic, ginger, cumin seeds, and black pepper in a light stock, then blending and straining the mixture to produce a smooth, thin broth. Fresh coriander leaves (dhania) are added at the end to impart a bright, herbaceous flavour. The resulting soup is earthy, mildly tangy from the tomatoes, lightly spiced, and fragrant with coriander. It is typically served hot in a small bowl or cup as a pre-meal appetiser. Nutritionally, the shorba is a low-calorie, low-fat, low-carbohydrate preparation (approximately 28 kcal per 100 g) with a meaningful sodium content from added salt and stock. The tomato base provides lycopene, vitamin C, and vitamin A. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index (~35) and very low glycemic load per serving (~3.3), making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content (~210 mg/100 g) is the primary clinical consideration for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. The dish is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-compatible. It is commonly found on the menus of North Indian restaurants in Singapore, including Curry Village, Bombay Curry and Kebabs, Aryana, and Zaffron Tandoori.
common_user_queries: Is tamatar dhania shorba good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Tamatar Dhaniya Shorba exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN tomato-based soup data and USDA SR Legacy tomato soup (FDC 172952), adjusted for the lighter North Indian shorba style. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database tomato soup entry. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, 2017) tomato soup composite and USDA SR Legacy FDC 172952 'Soup, tomato, home-prepared', adjusted for the thinner, less cream-enriched North Indian shorba preparation style. 2. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database tomato soup entry and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for a plant-based broth with no hydrogenated fats; naturally zero by ingredient composition. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as the dish is plant-based with no animal-derived fat in the standard vegetarian preparation. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based broth with no animal products. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as no vitamin D sources are present in the ingredients. 7. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy source data for this food category. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.4), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.2) are estimated from the fatty acid profile of the small quantity of oil or ghee used in tempering, consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for tomato soup. 9. The dish is served at 4 North Indian restaurants on field survey data (Curry Village, Bombay Curry and Kebabs, Aryana, Zaffron Tandoori) at SGD 9.90–11.90 per serving. 10. Dietary flag is_vegan set to false as ghee may be used in some restaurant preparations; is_vegetarian is true for the standard preparation.

Applied Singaporean dish multilingual naming rule (English, Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency.
